food_subgroup: Chinese sweet soup (tong sui) — gelatinous dessert

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for peach gum tong sui. FOODON_03311737 (sweet soup food product) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (sweet soups and dessert soups) is the closest parent. No specific code exists for peach gum tong sui in the FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for peach gum tong sui or analogous Chinese sweet soup dessert.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of peach gum tong sui is approximately 300 g, served warm or at room temperature as a dessert or light snack.

nutrient_source.title: Estimated composite from USDA FoodData Central ingredient data: peach gum (analogous to gelatin/collagen-rich resin), Tremella fuciformis (white fungus), lotus seeds, red dates, rock sugar, water; cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Tables for Southeast Asian sweet soups
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for peach gum tong sui with white fungus and lotus seed exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, or FAO/INFOODS SEA tables. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of individual ingredient data: peach gum resin (collagen-type polysaccharide, low protein, negligible fat, moderate carbohydrate from polysaccharides), white fungus Tremella fuciformis (USDA FDC #168436 analogous dried fungus rehydrated), lotus seeds (USDA FDC #169275), red dates/jujube (USDA FDC #168151), rock sugar (sucrose), and water. Typical recipe proportions used: peach gum 8 g dry (yields ~40 g rehydrated per 300 g bowl), white fungus 5 g dry (~50 g rehydrated), lotus seeds 20 g, red dates 15 g, rock sugar 20 g, water to 300 g. Values scaled to per-100 g basis. Confidence is medium due to estimation.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of sweet soups and sugar-sweetened gelatinous desserts — analogous estimate based on rock sugar (sucrose GI ~65) and lotus seed starch (GI ~40–55) composite; reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for peach gum tong sui with white fungus and lotus seed. GI estimated at 55 (low) based on: (1) rock sugar (sucrose) GI ~65 per University of Sydney GI Database; (2) lotus seed starch GI ~40–55 per peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al. 2008 International Tables); (3) white fungus and peach gum contribute primarily soluble polysaccharides with low glycemic impact. Weighted composite yields estimated GI of ~55. Glycemic load per 300 g serving = (55 × 42.6 g carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = 23.4, rounded to 23.5.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (~8 mg per 100 g; ~24 mg per 300 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Peach gum tong sui is naturally very low in sodium (~24 mg per serving), making it a suitable occasional dessert for individuals managing hypertension, provided added salt is avoided and portion size is controlled.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~55) but significant sugar content from rock sugar (~11.5 g sugar per 100 g; ~34.5 g per 300 g serving)
type2_diabetes.guidance: The substantial rock sugar content and estimated low GI (~55) mean that individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to half a bowl (~150 g) and consider requesting reduced sugar preparation to moderate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible saturated fat and cholesterol-free; white fungus (Tremella fuciformis) contains beta-glucan polysaccharides with potential lipid-modulating properties
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Peach gum tong sui is very low in saturated fat and contains no cholesterol; white fungus beta-glucans may offer modest cardiovascular benefit, though the added sugar content warrants moderation for individuals with cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Peach Gum Tong Sui with White Fungus and Lotus Seed (桃胶白木耳莲子糖水) is a traditional Chinese slow-cooked sweet dessert soup (tong sui) popular in Singapore, Malaysia, and southern China. The dish centres on peach gum — a natural resin harvested from wild peach trees (Prunus persica) — which is soaked overnight until it swells into translucent, gelatinous globules with a springy, smooth, and lightly chewy texture. Peach gum is rich in collagen-type polysaccharides and is prized in Chinese wellness culture for its purported skin-nourishing and moisturising properties. It is paired with white fungus (snow fungus, Tremella fuciformis), which contributes a delicate, crunchy-gelatinous texture and soluble beta-glucan polysaccharides associated with immune modulation and lipid management. Premium Wuyi Xuan lotus seeds (莲子) add a mild, starchy sweetness and a soft, floury bite, and are traditionally valued for their calming and digestive properties. Red dates (jujube, 红枣) infuse the broth with a natural sweetness and a warm, fruity depth, while rock sugar provides clean sweetness without the bitterness of refined white sugar. The soup is simmered over low heat for an extended period to allow the ingredients to meld and the peach gum to fully hydrate. Nutritionally, the dish is low in fat and protein but moderate in carbohydrate and sugar due to the rock sugar content. The estimated glycemic index is approximately 55 (low), making portion control important for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. Sodium content is very low, making it suitable for hypertension management. The dish is vegetarian, vegan, gluten-free, and dairy-free. It is commonly consumed as a dessert after a meal or as an afternoon snack, and is associated with beauty and wellness traditions in Chinese culinary culture.

confidence_reason: No direct entry for this specific dish exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA tables. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of individual ingredient data using USDA FoodData Central as the primary reference. GI is an analogous estimate based on component ingredients. Confidence is medium rather than low because the ingredient composition is well-documented and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted ingredient composite (peach gum resin, white fungus, lotus seeds, red dates, rock sugar, water) scaled to per-100 g basis; no direct analytical measurement of this dish was found in any approved primary source. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reported in the component ingredient sources used for estimation. vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the component ingredient sources. vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the component ingredient sources. vitamin_b3_niacin_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the component ingredient sources. vitamin_b6_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the component ingredient sources. vitamin_b12_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the component ingredient sources. folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in the component ingredient sources. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the component ingredient sources. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the component ingredient sources. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the component ingredient sources. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero — no animal fats or partially hydrogenated oils are used in this plant-based dessert). cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero — all ingredients are plant-derived). GI estimated at 55 (low) from analogous component analysis; direct GI measurement for this dish is not available. Serving size of 300 g is a typical restaurant bowl portion based on standard tong sui serving conventions. The dish is classified as Singaporean Chinese cuisine for multilingual naming purposes, requiring English, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi local names.
